one_line_takeaway: RBC watermarks LLM text by embedding an error-correcting code into a distortion-free binary sampling channel keyed off a sliding window of prior tokens, delivering deletion-robust detection (67.9% at 150 tokens vs 0% for the concurrent PRC method) while matching KGW-strength detection without KGW's quality loss.
key_finding: RBC with the LDPC code matches the state-of-the-art high-distortion KGW watermark on long-text detection power while remaining distortion-free, and beats it on short text and on p-value magnitude. On clean 150-token Llama-3-8B text, LDPC RBC detects 80.4% of generations at alpha=1e-6 (mean p 1.1e-23), just under KGW delta=2 at 85.8% but far above the distortion-free EXP-Edit baseline at 30.5%; at 30 tokens LDPC RBC is the best method at 34.4% detection. The LDPC (error-correcting) variant consistently beats the one-to-one (no-correction) variant, isolating the benefit of the ECC. Robustness is the strongest selling point: under 20% deletion at 150 tokens, LDPC RBC holds 67.9% detection while the concurrent PRC method collapses to 0.0% (deletions destroy its fixed-codeword alignment). RBC also leads or ties the baselines under swap, translation, and paraphrase perturbations, and preserves generation quality (low perplexity), whereas KGW delta=2's detection strength comes at the cost of visibly higher perplexity.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the Robust Binary Code (RBC) watermark for detecting machine-generated text. The distinguishing contribution over prior work is combining (1) error-correcting codes (ECCs), primarily an LDPC code, with (2) a sliding-window, text-dependent key derived from previously generated tokens. The binary sampling channel it uses (the Correlated Binary Sampling Channel, CBSC) is credited to Christ and Gunn (2024) and was developed independently/concurrently by the authors; RBC's novelty is not the channel itself but layering ECC redundancy on top of a per-block message hashed from a sliding window of prior tokens. This sliding-window design is what buys robustness to deletions, in contrast to the concurrent PRC method (Christ and Gunn 2024) which uses fixed-length codeword strings from a secret key and thus loses alignment when tokens are deleted. The paper also separates its goal from Qu et al. (2024), who also use ECCs but to embed a multi-bit payload (e.g., a user ID); RBC targets the single-bit 'is this text watermarked' detection problem. The method is next-token distortion-free (preserves the model's per-step output distribution), and the paper adds an information-theoretic analysis tying detectability to the language model's entropy, plus a Binomial Comparison test that yields calibrated p-values.
threat_model: Detection from the model provider's perspective. The detector receives only the candidate text (user- or machine-generated) with no metadata, prompt, or generation parameters, and must classify it as watermarked or not while controlling false positives (evaluated at alpha=1e-6, with empirical FPR shown to track the p-value threshold). The adversary is a user who wants to evade detection by post-editing model output. The paper evaluates four non-adaptive, model-agnostic evasion transformations at a 20% perturbation rate: random deletion, random token swap/substitution, round-trip translation (English->Russian->English via Argos Translate), and paraphrasing (via the same generating model, and additionally GPT-4o in the appendix). The authors explicitly scope OUT adaptive, watermark-aware attacks (e.g., watermark stealing/spoofing, smoothing attacks), noting these are a separate line of work.
limitations: Only non-adaptive, model-agnostic attacks are tested; adaptive watermark-aware attacks (stealing, spoofing, smoothing) are explicitly out of scope, so real adversarial robustness is unestablished. Distortion-freeness holds only per next-token / per-block, NOT over the whole generation: the appendix concedes the pseudorandom key's entropy degrades when past tokens have low entropy, so it does not achieve steganographic (Cachin-1998) perfect security. Detectability is fundamentally entropy-limited by the theory (low-entropy models/prompts are hard to watermark). On clean long text RBC does not surpass KGW delta=2 in raw detection %. Evaluation is limited to a few open models, 10 prompts, and mostly Llama-3-8B; only two ECCs (LDPC, one-to-one) are explored, with BCH/Turbo/Reed-Solomon left to future work. The practical decoder is belief propagation on a BSC (p=0.35), an approximation to the true MAP detector under the CBSC (the optimal detector would need access to the model's bit probabilities at detection time, which is impractical).

datasets_used: 10 hand-crafted long-form generation prompts (book reports, stories, fake-news articles) inspired by Piet et al. 2025; 100 generations per prompt at 7 lengths from 10 to 150 tokens
quantitative_results: Clean detection @150 tokens (Detect % at alpha=1e-6): LDPC RBC 80.4, One-to-one RBC 76.6, PRC 75.7, KGW delta=2 85.8, Token Specific 79.6, KGW delta=1 13.8, EXP-Edit 30.5
Clean detection @30 tokens (Detect %): LDPC RBC 34.4 (best), One-to-one RBC 30.5, PRC 28.8, EXP-Edit 16.1, KGW delta=2 14.9, Token Specific 12.4, KGW delta=1 0.2
Clean mean p-value @150 tokens: LDPC RBC 1.1e-23 (best), One-to-one RBC 1.5e-18, PRC 1.3e-15, KGW delta=2 2.1e-14, Token Specific 3.5e-13, EXP-Edit 5.2e-2
Deletion 20% @150 tokens (Detect %): LDPC RBC 67.9 (best), KGW delta=2 64.2, One-to-one RBC 60.4, Token Specific 51.1, KGW delta=1 4.6, PRC 0.0 (complete failure)
Swap 20% @150 tokens (Detect %): LDPC RBC 59.0 (best), One-to-one RBC 51.3, KGW delta=2 49.8, PRC 41.5, Token Specific 35.1, KGW delta=1 1.7
Translate @150 tokens (Detect %): LDPC RBC 48.6 (best), One-to-one RBC 44.5, KGW delta=2 35.5, Token Specific 23.1, KGW delta=1 1.2, PRC 0.8
Paraphrase @150 tokens (Detect %): LDPC RBC 37.6 (best), One-to-one RBC 37.2, KGW delta=2 31.9, Token Specific 28.2, PRC 15.8, KGW delta=1 1.0
Theory: E[dH(B,Y)/n] bounded in [(1-h)/2, sqrt(1-h^2)/2], decreasing in avg model entropy h -> 0 as h -> 1 (Thm 4.3)
baselines_compared: EXP-Edit (Kuditipudi et al. 2023; exponential-minimum-sampling distortion-free watermark, 256 keys, permutation test with 100 resamples)
PRC / Pseudorandom Error-Correcting Codes (Christ and Gunn 2024; same LDPC settings, fixed-codeword key)
KGW delta=1 (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023; green/red list, medium distortion shift)
KGW delta=2 (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023; strong distortion shift, cited as practical SOTA)
Token Specific (Huo et al. 2024; neural-predicted per-token delta/gamma KGW variant)
One-to-one RBC (paper's own no-error-correction ablation, C(x)=x XOR R, n=k=4)
